title: A Word to Fathers
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: A Word to Fathers - 1 Thessalonians 2_10-12 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Crisis of Fatherhood
subpoints: The family in our society is bankrupt, and in large part the blame falls on the shoulders of fathers
The evangelical church has not fared much better, having bought into worldly ideas about masculinity and fatherhood
Fathers can turn things around in one generation if they commit themselves to what God says in his Word
Paul's dealings with the Thessalonians as a spiritual father provide divine insights into what it means to be a father

outline.title: The Goal of Every Father (v. 12)
subpoints: Paul's stated goal: 'that you would walk worthy of God who calls you into his own kingdom and glory'
George Barna research: the top outcome desired by parents in evangelical churches is a good education (4 in 10), not walking worthy of God
A good education is meaningless in hell — the ultimate goal must be to help children walk worthy of their God
The Word of God is the plumb line for all fathering: it tells us if our parenting is 'plumb' — straight, level, square
The goal must govern everything else: choices about influences, activities, time, and priorities

outline.title: The Conduct of the Father (v. 10)
subpoints: Paul describes his own conduct: 'devoutly and justly and blamelessly we behaved ourselves among you'
Children must see in us a true devotion to God that supersedes devotion to anything else in life
They must witness righteousness: moral conduct dictated by the Word of God, not personal preference
Blamelessness is not perfection but a clear conscience — acting rightly so that accusations have no ground
Jonathan Edwards: every family ought to be a little church consecrated to Christ, and the father is its pastor

outline.title: The Teaching of the Father (v. 11)
subpoints: Paul says he dealt with each one individually: 'as a father does his own children' — fathering must be tailored to each child
Three activities: (1) Exhortation — impressing instruction on someone with the goal of practice; each father is pastor to his family
Three activities: (2) Comfort — speaking in a friendly way, coming alongside, putting your arm around and asking how they are doing; the word PARA implies proximity
Three activities: (3) Charging — an emphatic demand to put into practice what was taught; this is where discipline comes in
Fathers must be present: you cannot exhort, comfort, or charge from a distance

outline.title: The Rod and Biblical Discipline
subpoints: Proverbs: 'Do not withhold correction from a child, for if you beat him with a rod, he will not die; you shall beat him with a rod and deliver his soul from hell'
Spanking has become unacceptable even in the church, yet Scripture is clear: 'He who spares his rod hates his son, but he who loves him disciplines him promptly'
The mystical union between the child's bottom and their heart — the rod is an agent of their salvation
Refusing to spank because 'it doesn't work' is the same logic as refusing to preach the gospel because a neighbor hasn't been saved yet
The words 'wait till your father gets home' should strike reverence, not terror — because father is serious about his children's souls

outline.title: The Heavenly Father as Our Pattern and Hope
subpoints: God himself does these same things for his people: he exhorts, comforts, and charges us as a father does his children
For fathers under conviction who don't know how to begin: look at your Heavenly Father, learn from him, be a reflection of him
For those who never had a godly earthly father: your Heavenly Father is doing these things for you right now
Psalm 27:10: 'When my father and my mother forsake me, then the LORD will take care of me'
Fathers can turn things around in one generation if they commit to the Word of God, empowered by the Spirit, through the sacrifice of Christ

summary:
Preached on the occasion of a baptism in the congregation, Pastor Tito Lyro directs a word to fathers — and to all who have a stake in raising the next generation — from 1 Thessalonians 2:10-12. Paul's ministry to the Thessalonians served as the pattern of a faithful father: he had a clear goal for them, he conducted himself before them with integrity, and he taught them through exhortation, comfort, and emphatic charge.

The sermon opens with a frank assessment of the crisis of fatherhood in both culture and church. Citing George Barna's research, Pastor Lyro notes that four out of ten evangelical parents list a good education as their top goal for their children — while facilitating a meaningful relationship with Christ ranks third. The biblical goal is clear: that children would walk worthy of God who calls them into his kingdom and glory. The Word of God must function as the plumb line for all fathering decisions.

Paul's conduct before the Thessalonians (v. 10) models what children must see in their fathers: genuine devotion to God, righteousness governed by Scripture, and blamelessness that produces a clear conscience. His teaching (v. 11) involved three activities that must be present in every father's relationship with each child individually: exhortation (instruction aimed at practice), comfort (coming alongside with encouragement and genuine pastoral care), and charging (the emphatic demand that produces obedience, enforced through biblical discipline). The sermon addresses spanking directly and without apology, grounding it in Proverbs and noting that the pastoral reluctance to apply the rod is based on cultural accommodation rather than Scripture.

The sermon closes by pointing fathers under conviction to the Heavenly Father as both model and hope. God does the very things for his people that Paul describes, and those who had no faithful earthly father can look to him as the perfect Father who never abandons his children.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 1 Thessalonians 2:10-12

If you are able, remain standing for the Scripture reading. We are turning to the book of 1 Thessalonians chapter 2. I am going to read verses 10, 11, and 12.

*You are witnesses, and God also, how devoutly and justly and blamelessly we behaved ourselves among you who believe; as you know how we exhorted, and comforted, and charged every one of you as a father does his own children, that you would walk worthy of God who calls you into His own kingdom and glory.*

Let us pray together. Father, thank you for your Word. We pray that you speak to us through it. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: The Crisis of Fatherhood

On the occasion of this baptism, I want to take the opportunity to share a word with the new father and with all the fathers present here. And I want everyone to listen to this word — if you are a mother who has boys at home, you are going to be very responsible for shaping them into what we are discussing today. If you are a man who is looking forward to marriage and to being a father, this is for you. The women without children at home still need to pray — so listen closely so you know what to pray for. There is something here for everybody, because this is the Word of God, and the Word of God always accomplishes what God sends it to accomplish.

The family in our society is bankrupt. And in large part, the blame lays squarely on the shoulders of the father. Sadly, the family in the evangelical church is not very much better than the family in the world. For years we have been teaching our sons to be more like women, which in turn has produced fathers who are more like mothers. Those who take their calling as fathers seriously are often at a loss, because the church has bought into worldly ideas about what being a father means. Even the evangelical church has bought into the idea of toxic masculinity, a blanket expression that covers anything to do with being a biblically informed male.

Is there abuse in the church of Jesus Christ? Yes, and that is very sad. We are not immune to the presence of sin in our lives, and we need to repent and the church needs to deal seriously with that. But to throw masculinity out the window because of that is to throw the Bible out of the church.

The job of the pastor and teacher, according to Ephesians 4:11-12, is to equip the saints to do the work of the ministry — including raising our children to the glory of God. Yet the pastor has become an entertainer while the families in the pews are laughing their way to broken families, heartache, and suffering.

But it does not have to be that way. Fathers — I firmly believe this — we can turn things around in one generation if we commit ourselves to what God says in his Word. It is as simple as that. If we men are willing to be men as the Bible tells us men should be, we can turn things around in the church of Jesus Christ in just one generation. But it takes courage on your part to be a real man — a man who will follow Jesus Christ and bring his family with him.

Paul in his dealings with the Thessalonians provides great divine insights into what it is to be a father. He tells us what every father's goal should be. He tells us the means to achieve that goal. And he shows us that our Heavenly Father does the same for us.

## The Goal of Every Father (v. 12)

Verse 12 tells us the goal. Paul says: *that you would walk worthy of God who calls you into His own kingdom and glory.* That is the goal. Paul ministered to the Thessalonians as a father so that they would be able to walk worthy of God for his glory.

So let me ask you, fathers: what is it that you want for your children? A good education? A scholarship for college? To stay out of jail? To be a loving, well-adjusted person?

George Barna, a Christian statistician, conducted a recent poll of parents in evangelical churches about their top goals for their children. The result: four out of every ten parents in the church listed a good education as the number one critical outcome they were committed to facilitating. What is the problem with that? Does it matter how educated you are in hell? The second result: 24% wanted their kids to feel loved. And third — almost an afterthought — 22% listed facilitating a meaningful relationship with Jesus Christ.

Isn't that backwards? Paul tells us that a father must want his children to walk worthy of their God. The word *walk* designates the whole of a person's life. If that is our goal, we must use the Bible as the plumb line for everything we do as a father. Do you know what a plumb line is? It is a string with a weight at the bottom that you hold against things to see if they are straight and vertical. The Word of God is that instrument. Fathers, we need to grab the Word of God and hold it against our parenting and ask: is this straight? Is it square? Is it level?

How are your decisions concerning your children — concerning influences, activities, your wife, your family — going to make them worthy of walking with God? Whatever else goes on in parenting takes second place to helping our children walk worthy of their God. And this must go beyond words — it will affect what we do, how we spend our time, and everything else. Because this cannot be our goal for our children if it is not our goal for ourselves.

## The Conduct of the Father (v. 10)

Verse 10: *You are witnesses, and God also, how devoutly and justly and blamelessly we behaved ourselves among you who believe.*

Paul's conduct before the Thessalonians was witnessed both by them and by God. He says there are three things they witnessed: devoutness, righteousness, and blamelessness. So let me ask you, fathers: what do your children witness in the day-to-day of your life? What does God witness?

R.C. Sproul made famous the phrase *coram Deo* — before the face of God. Do you realize that we live, every moment, before the face of God? There is nowhere where God is not present. What is God witnessing in your day-to-day life?

In his behavior as a father to the Thessalonians, Paul tells us what our children must see in us.

**Devotion to God** — our children must see in us a true devotion to God that supersedes devotion to anything else in life. Our lives must be marked by being set apart unto God. Is that your life, Father?

**Righteousness** — this speaks of our attitude toward the law of God. We must cherish it and follow it. It must govern our moral conduct. Is the Word of God dictating what you do, Father? Are you putting in the time needed to know the Word of God for yourself and for your family?

Jonathan Edwards, in his very last sermon in Northampton — he was the greatest mind the United States ever produced — said: *Every family ought to be a little church consecrated to Christ and under holy influence and governed by his rules. Family education and order are some of the chief means of grace. If these fail, all other means are likely to prove ineffectual.* And who is the pastor of that little church? The father — the head of the household.

**Blamelessness** — this is not perfection, but a clear conscience. It is the idea of doing what God calls you to do such that your conscience is clear. As you have interacted with your children, is your conscience clear because of the way you have raised them? If not, repent, confess, and experience the forgiveness of the Lord.

## The Teaching of the Father (v. 11)

Verse 11: *As you know how we exhorted, and comforted, and charged every one of you as a father does his own children.*

Notice that he says *every one of you*. Our fathering must be tailored for each one of our children. Of course, some things are the same for all the kids — we want to avoid favoritism — but we must be a father to each individual child. I am not just the kids' father. I am each one's father. And that is what Paul did — he dealt with each one of the Thessalonians individually.

Paul's dealings with the Thessalonians involved three activities that must be present in our dealings with our children.

**Exhortation** — the word means to impress something upon someone, to direct them into suitable conduct, to instruct with the goal of practicing. This is the teaching activity of the three. Each father here is, in this sense, a pastor to his family. Ephesians 6:4 gives the command specifically to fathers: raise your children in the training and admonition of the Lord.

**Comfort** — the basic sense of this word is to speak to someone in a friendly way about what ought to be done — to encourage and to mend. Notice that both of these words — exhort and comfort — have the prefix *para*, which implies proximity: coming alongside. You cannot exhort and encourage from a distance. Father, you must be present in your children's lives. You must be in the trenches with them.

**Charging** — to urge, to insist, to demand that what was taught be put into practice. This is where discipline comes in. We are to make an emphatic demand that our children walk worthy of their God — and that is often done by discipline applied to the backside.

Spanking has become unacceptable, even in the church of Jesus Christ. And yet the Scriptures have told us that the most effective way to correct a young child's behavior is with the rod. *Do not withhold correction from a child, for if you beat him with a rod, he will not die; you shall beat him with a rod and deliver his soul from hell.* The Bible teaches: if you spare the rod, you hate your children. There is a mystical union between the child's bottom and their heart. *He who spares his rod hates his son, but he who loves him disciplines him promptly. The rod and rebuke give wisdom, but a child left to himself brings shame to his mother.*

Fathers, the words 'wait till your father gets home' should strike reverence in your children — not because when you get home you are going to throw a temper tantrum, but because they know that Daddy is serious about their soul.

## The Heavenly Father as Our Pattern and Hope

Paul finishes by reminding us that in Christ Jesus, we have a Heavenly Father who does the same for us. Verse 12: *that you would walk worthy of God, who calls you into His own kingdom and glory.*

Let me say something to fathers who, after hearing all these things, are under tremendous conviction but feel they do not know where to begin: look at your Heavenly Father. See where the word 'father' is used of God in Scripture and see what he is doing, because he is doing those same things for you. Learn from him. Be a reflection of him.

And let me say something to those who never had a father like the one I have described — or who realize they have not been the father I have described: your Heavenly Father is doing these things for you. Even if your earthly father has failed you, your Heavenly Father has not failed you. In him you find the perfect Father.

Psalm 27:10: *When my father and my mother forsake me, then the LORD will take care of me.* The Scriptures say: *Your Father knows the things you have need of before you ask him. Behold what manner of love the Father has bestowed on us, that we should be called children of God. Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction.* That is your Father in heaven.

Fathers, we can turn things around in one generation. I really believe that. If you men are committed to the Word of God, delivered to you by the Spirit of God who enables you to obey the Son of God by the sacrifice of Jesus Christ — we can turn things around in one generation. But it takes courage on your part, fathers, to be a real man — a man who will follow Jesus Christ and bring his family with him.

Are you going to be that man? I pray that we will.

Father, grant us the grace to be obedient to you. We thank you that your Spirit is already at work in us to make us more like the Lord Jesus Christ. We are blessed to stand on the truth of your Word and to be faithful men in our families, our society, and our church. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
